items.title: Know whether your problem is convex
items.avoid: Running expensive hyperparameter sweeps without first checking if the formulation is convex (e.g., linear/logistic regression).
items.prefer: Identify convex baselines; if convex, a single well-tuned run should suffice; if non-convex, budget multiple seeds and track validation.
items.why: Convexity determines whether you need search over initializations or can trust one optimum.
items.example: Before tuning 50 seeds on logistic regression, confirm the loss is convex—one seed with line search on learning rate is enough.

items.title: Diagnose critical points with curvature
items.avoid: Stopping solely because ||gradient|| is tiny on a non-convex loss.
items.prefer: When debugging, inspect Hessian eigenvalues (or use optimizers with saddle-escaping properties) at suspicious plateaus.
items.why: Zero gradient at a saddle looks like convergence but loss can still decrease along negative-curvature directions.
items.example: If loss stalls near 0.3 with gradient norm 1e-6, sample a few Hessian eigenvalues; negative values suggest escaping directions remain.
